## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Define engineered nanomaterial as per EU Novel Food Regulation 2015/2283. Why is a separate regulatory trigger required for food applications?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** An *engineered nanomaterial* is any intentionally produced material having one or more dimensions of the order of **100 nm or less**, or which is composed of discrete functional parts, either internally or at the surface, many of which have one or more dimensions of the order of **100 nm or less**, including structures, agglomerates, or aggregates that may have a size above 100 nm but retain nano-scale properties.
>
> **Why separate trigger (1.5 marks):** Nanomaterials exhibit *size-dependent* properties — increased surface area, altered bioavailability, and potential to cross biological barriers (gut, blood-brain, placental). Conventional bulk-material safety data **cannot be extrapolated** to their nano-forms. Hence, regulators require nano-specific toxicological, exposure, and labeling data before approval.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**List any four global regulatory bodies involved in nanofood governance and state their primary focus.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Body | Primary Focus |
|---|------|---------------|
| 1 | **US FDA** | Pre-market approval of food additives and food contact substances containing nanomaterials |
| 2 | **EFSA (EU)** | Risk assessment under the Novel Food Regulation; mandatory labeling |
| 3 | **FSSAI (India)** | Indian draft Nano-Food Regulations, 2022; licensing and labeling |
| 4 | **OECD WPMN** | International test guidelines for nanomaterial safety testing |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the major regulatory frameworks governing nanofood in the **United States (FDA)**, **European Union (EFSA)**, and **India (FSSAI)**. Compare their approaches to pre-market approval and labeling. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **risk assessment methodology** used for engineered nanomaterials in food, with reference to the risk equation, dose metrics, and uncertainty factors. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Question A:**

**(a) Major Regulatory Frameworks — 7 marks**

**United States FDA (2 marks):**
- Governed by the **Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act (FFDCA)**.
- No separate law for nanotechnology — regulated as **food additives**, **GRAS substances**, or **food contact substances**.
- Draft guidance (2014) recommends considering **size, surface properties, and bioactivity** for any material at or below **100 nm**.
- **GRAS self-affirmation** by manufacturer is permitted but may be challenged post-market.

**European Union EFSA (2 marks):**
- **Regulation (EU) 2015/2283 (Novel Food)** explicitly mentions engineered nanomaterials — requires mandatory pre-market authorization.
- **EFSA's 2011 Scientific Opinion** requires nano-specific data on physico-chemical characterization, toxicity, and exposure.
- **Mandatory labeling** with the term "nano" in brackets after the ingredient.
- All novel foods containing ENMs undergo a **case-by-case assessment**.

**India FSSAI (2 marks):**
- **Food Safety and Standards (Food or Food Ingredients Obtained through Certain Processing Techniques involving Nanotechnology) Regulations, 2022** (draft).
- Requires **pre-market approval** and **product license**.
- All ingredients containing ENMs must carry the prefix **"nano"** on the label.
- Mandatory **post-market surveillance** and **adverse event reporting**.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | USA (FDA) | EU (EFSA) | India (FSSAI) |
|--------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| Separate nano law? | No | Yes (Novel Food) | Yes (Draft 2022) |
| Pre-market approval | Conditional (additives/FCN) | Mandatory for novel | Mandatory |
| Labeling requirement | Case-by-case | Mandatory "nano" | Mandatory "nano" |
| Post-market surveillance | Voluntary | Mandatory | Mandatory |

---

**(b) Risk Assessment Methodology — 7 marks**

**Step 1: Hazard Identification (1 mark)**
Identification of intrinsic toxic properties — genotoxicity, cytotoxicity, immunotoxicity, etc.

**Step 2: Hazard Characterization / Dose-Response (1.5 marks)**
Determination of NOAEL (No Observed Adverse Effect Level) or BMDL.

**Step 3: Exposure Assessment (1.5 marks)**
Estimation of daily intake via food:
$$
EDI = \frac{C \times CR}{bw}
$$
where $C$ = concentration, $CR$ = consumption rate, $bw$ = body weight.

**Step 4: Risk Characterization (1.5 marks)**
$$
MOE = \frac{BMDL}{EDI}
$$
Decision rule: $MOE \geq 10000$ → acceptable.

**Step 5: Uncertainty Factors (1 mark)**
- Interspecies variability (×10)
- Intraspecies variability (×10)
- Additional factor (×10) for nano-specific uncertainties (size, agglomeration, etc.)

**Step 6: Risk Management (0.5 mark)**
- Risk = Hazard × Exposure
- Reformulation, labeling, or rejection based on outcome.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the **FSSAI draft regulations on nanotechnology in food (2022)**. What are the labelling and packaging requirements for nano-food products? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **OECD test guidelines** used for safety evaluation of engineered nanomaterials in food. Why are conventional test guidelines insufficient? *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer — Question B:**

**(a) FSSAI Draft Regulations 2022 — 7 marks**

**Scope (2 marks):**
- Applicable to all food business operators (FBOs) using engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) in food ingredients, additives, processing aids, or food contact materials (FCMs).
- Threshold: any material with **one or more dimensions ≤ 100 nm**.

**Pre-market Approval (2 marks):**
- Submission of technical dossier including physico-chemical characterization, toxicological data, intended use, and proposed levels.
- Evaluation by a **scientific panel** appointed by FSSAI.
- Issuance of **product license** upon satisfactory review.

**Labeling Requirements (2 marks):**
- The word **"nano"** must precede the name of the ingredient (e.g., "nano-selenium").
- Quantitative declaration of the nanomaterial.
- Allergen and safety information.

**Packaging Requirements (1 mark):**
- Migration studies must be conducted for FCMs containing ENMs.
- Specific Migration Limit (SML) must not be exceeded.

---

**(b) OECD Test Guidelines and Their Limitations — 7 marks**

**Key OECD Test Guidelines (3 marks):**

| Test Guideline | Purpose |
|----------------|---------|
| **OECD TG 403** | Acute inhalation toxicity |
| **OECD TG 425** | Acute oral toxicity — Up-and-Down Procedure |
| **OECD TG 407** | Repeated dose 28-day oral toxicity in rodents |
| **OECD TG 408** | 90-day oral toxicity |
| **OECD TG 487** | In vitro micronucleus (genotoxicity) |
| **OECD TG 471** | Bacterial reverse mutation |

**Why Conventional TGs Are Insufficient (4 marks):**

1. **Size & Surface Effects:** Standard TG endpoints are designed for soluble chemicals — they ignore surface-area-driven reactivity of nanoparticles.
2. **Agglomeration Behavior:** ENMs behave differently in test matrices vs. real food — they may agglomerate, de-agglomerate, or transform.
3. **Biodistribution:** Nanoparticles can cross biological barriers (gut-associated lymphoid tissue, placenta, BBB) — not addressed in classical toxicology.
4. **Dose Metric Confusion:** Should dose be expressed as **mass**, **surface area**, or **particle number**? Classical TGs use only mass.
5. **Need for Adapted TGs:** OECD's Working Party on Manufactured Nanomaterials (WPMN) is developing **modified test guidelines** specifically for ENMs.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "size threshold"** — Students often write 1000 nm instead of 100 nm. Always remember the *100 nm* rule.
> 2. **Omitting the "nano" labelling prefix** — A mandatory requirement under FSSAI 2022 and EU 2015/2283.
> 3. **Stating "nanoparticles are GRAS"** — GRAS applies to specific substances, not generic nanoparticle classes.
> 4. **Mixing up FDA and EFSA approaches** — FDA has *no* separate nano-law; EFSA does (Novel Food Regulation).
> 5. **Skipping uncertainty factors** — A complete answer must mention the ×100 default UF and the additional ×10 for nano-uncertainties.
> 6. **Not mentioning OECD WPMN** — Examiners often award bonus marks for international harmonization awareness.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 – Regulatory Aspects: Quick-Fire Recap**

- **Definition:** Regulatory aspects = laws, guidelines, and protocols controlling nanofood safety across the chain.
- **Three pillars:** Characterization, Risk Assessment, Transparency (labeling).
- **Key equation:** $Risk = Hazard \times Exposure$.
- **Nano threshold:** $d \leq 100 \text{ nm}$ (one or more dimensions).
- **US FDA:** No separate nano-law; uses FFDCA, GRAS, FCN pathways.
- **EU EFSA:** Novel Food Regulation 2015/2283; mandatory "nano" labelling.
- **India FSSAI:** Draft 2022 Regulations; mandatory "nano" prefix; product license required.
- **OECD WPMN:** Develops nanomaterial-adapted test guidelines.
- **Mass-to-number dose conversion:** $D_N = \dfrac{6 \cdot D_m}{\rho \pi d^3 \cdot bw}$.
- **SSA formula:** $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho d}$ — increases as size decreases.
- **MOE rule:** $MOE \geq 10000$ = acceptable risk.
- **UF default:** $100$ (×10 inter-species × ×10 intra-species) + ×10 nano-factor.
- **Labelling:** Always prefix with "nano"; declare quantity.
- **Codex/FAO/WHO:** Provide global harmonization guidance.
- **Post-market surveillance:** Mandatory in EU and India; voluntary in US.
- **Common mistake:** Confusing mass dose with particle-number dose during risk assessment.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Risk Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT743 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Risk Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Risk Assessment (RA)** is a systematic, science-based, and internationally harmonized evaluation process used to estimate the probability and severity of adverse health effects in humans, animals, or the environment resulting from exposure to **engineered nanomaterials (ENMs)** intentionally introduced into the food chain (food additives, food contact materials, nutraceuticals, nano-encapsulated bioactives, or agro-chemicals).

In regulatory toxicology, Risk Assessment is formally defined as a four-step paradigm codified by the **National Research Council (NRC, 1983)** and adopted by the **European Food Safety Authority (EFSA)** and the **Codex Alimentarius Commission**:

$$ RA = \{ Hazard\ Identification \rightarrow Hazard\ Characterization \rightarrow Exposure\ Assessment \rightarrow Risk\ Characterization \} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** Risk Assessment in food nanotechnology is **not** the same as Risk *Management* (the regulatory decision-making step). RA produces the *scientific estimate*; Risk Management applies policy and controls. Examiners often test this distinction for **3 marks**.

### 1.2 The Key Master Equation

The fundamental relationship in all chemical/nanomaterial risk science is:

$$ Risk = Hazard \times Exposure $$

Where:
- **Hazard** = intrinsic toxicological property of the nanomaterial (e.g., ability to generate ROS, membrane disruption, genotoxicity).
- **Exposure** = the actual dose reaching the biological target (dose = concentration × time × absorption fraction).
- **Risk** = a dimensionless or qualitative probability of harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics You MUST Memorize:**
> - **NOAEL**: No Observed Adverse Effect Level — units = **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **LOAEL**: Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level — units = **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **ADI**: Acceptable Daily Intake — units = **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **TDI**: Tolerable Daily Intake — units = **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **RfD**: Reference Dose — units = **mg/kg body weight/day**
> - **UF**: Uncertainty Factor — dimensionless, default values = **10, 100, 1000**
> - **Standard body weight** for an adult in EFSA calculations = **70 kg**
> - **Standard body weight** for a child in EFSA calculations = **15 kg**
> - **PCC**: Product Conversion Coefficient for solid food matrices
> - **LOQ**: Limit of Quantification for analytical detection

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of Risk Assessment like **deciding whether it is safe to let a child swim in a new lake**:

| Lake Analogy | Nanotechnology in Food |
|---|---|
| Is the water poisonous? (crocodiles, algae, bacteria) | **Hazard Identification** — Is the ENM inherently toxic? (e.g., does TiO₂ generate ROS?) |
| How much poison per litre? | **Hazard Characterization** — What is the dose-response curve? (NOAEL, LOAEL) |
| How long will the child swim? How much water will be swallowed? | **Exposure Assessment** — How much nanomaterial will a consumer actually ingest daily? |
| Is the swim safe? Should the lake be closed? | **Risk Characterization** — Combining hazard × exposure → Margin of Exposure (MoE) decision |

If the lake has **zero crocodiles** (zero hazard), risk is **zero** regardless of swim time. If the crocodile density (hazard) is high but swim time (exposure) is one minute, the risk may still be **acceptable**. This is why both factors must be quantified separately.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** Whenever a KTU question asks *"Define risk assessment in the context of food nanotechnology,"* your answer **must** mention: (i) engineered nanomaterials, (ii) the four-step NRC framework, and (iii) the relationship **Risk = Hazard × Exposure**. Missing any of the three forfeits marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Nanomaterial Risk Assessment — Step-by-Step Logic

#### **Pillar 1: Hazard Identification**

*Why this step:* Before quantifying any risk, scientists must first establish *whether* the ENM has any intrinsic capacity to cause harm.

*How it works:*
1. **Physicochemical characterization** of the ENM (size, shape, surface charge/zeta potential, crystal structure, agglomeration state, dissolution rate, surface chemistry).
2. **In silico screening** (QSAR models, read-across from analogous materials).
3. **In vitro assays** (cytotoxicity — MTT/LDH assays, genotoxicity — comet assay, micronucleus, ROS generation via DCFH-DA probe, membrane integrity).
4. **In vivo studies** on rodents (acute, sub-chronic, chronic toxicity, reproductive toxicity, immunotoxicity).
5. **Omics profiling** (transcriptomics, proteomics, metabolomics for mechanism of action).
6. **Identification of endpoints**: carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, reproductive toxicity, nano-specific effects (e.g., frustrated phagocytosis, protein corona formation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Nano-Specific Hazard Identifiers (high-yield for KTU):**
> - **Frustrated Phagocytosis** — alveolar/interstitial macrophages cannot engulf fibers > 10 µm → granuloma
> - **Protein Corona** — biomolecules adsorb onto the ENM surface in vivo, altering its biological identity
> - **Trojan Horse Effect** — ENMs carry bound toxins across biological barriers (e.g., blood-brain barrier, gut epithelium)
> - **ROS Generation** — TiO₂, ZnO, Ag, CeO₂, SiO₂ ENMs can catalyze reactive oxygen species under UV or biological conditions

#### **Pillar 2: Hazard Characterization (Dose-Response)**

The output is a quantitative relationship between the dose (or concentration) of the ENM and the magnitude/incidence of an adverse response. Standard outputs:

$$ ADI = \frac{NOAEL\ (or\ LOAEL)}{UF_{inter-species} \times UF_{intra-species} \times UF_{other}} $$

$$ ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{100} \quad \text{(default inter + intra species uncertainty)} $$

The **NOAEL** is read from the dose-response curve at the dose below which no statistically or biologically significant increase in adverse effects is observed relative to the control.

#### **Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment**

Quantifies the *actual* amount of ENM entering the human body per unit body weight per day through all relevant routes:

$$ E_{daily} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (C_i \times IR_i \times ABS_i \times PCC_i) $$

Where:
- $C_i$ = concentration of ENM in food matrix $i$ (mg/kg food)
- $IR_i$ = ingestion rate of food $i$ (kg food/day)
- $ABS_i$ = oral absorption fraction (dimensionless, 0 to 1)
- $PCC_i$ = product conversion coefficient (correction for cooking/processing)

For chronic non-cancer risk, the **Average Daily Dose (ADD)** and **Lifetime Average Daily Dose (LADD)** are used. For acute risk, the **Single-Day Exposure (SDE)** is used.

#### **Pillar 4: Risk Characterization**

Final step that integrates Pillars 1–3 into a quantitative or qualitative risk estimate. Two parallel approaches are used:

**(a) Margin of Exposure (MoE) Approach** — used for non-threshold carcinogens:

$$ MoE = \frac{POD}{EDD} $$

Where $POD$ is the Point of Departure (e.g., BMDL₁₀ = Benchmark Dose Lower Confidence Limit at 10% effect) and $EDD$ is the Estimated Daily Dose.

**(b) Hazard Quotient (HQ) / Risk Quotient (RQ) Approach** — used for threshold effects:

$$ HQ = \frac{EDD}{ADI} $$

Decision rule: $HQ \leq 1$ → **Acceptable risk**; $HQ > 1$ → **Unacceptable risk**, requires risk management action.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Questions)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Standard Units | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Risk = Hazard \times Exposure$ | Probability of harm | dimensionless | Conceptual definitions |
| 2 | $ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{UF_{total}}$ | Acceptable Daily Intake | mg/kg bw/day | Threshold toxicants |
| 3 | $HQ = \frac{EDD}{ADI}$ | Hazard Quotient | dimensionless | Threshold risk |
| 4 | $MoE = \frac{BMDL_{10}}{EDD}$ | Margin of Exposure | dimensionless | Non-threshold (carcinogens) |
| 5 | $EDD = \frac{C \times IR \times ABS}{BW}$ | Estimated Daily Dose | mg/kg bw/day | All routes |
| 6 | $E_{total} = \sum C_i \cdot IR_i \cdot ABS_i$ | Aggregated exposure | mg/kg bw/day | Multi-food exposure |
| 7 | $UF = UF_A \times UF_H \times UF_L \times UF_D$ | Uncertainty Factor | dimensionless | ADI derivation |
| 8 | $BMD = \text{point estimate from dose-response fit}$ | Benchmark Dose | mg/kg bw/day | Modern Pillar 2 |
| 9 | $D_{particle} = \frac{6}{\rho \cdot SSA}$ | Equivalent spherical diameter | nm | ENM characterization |
| 10 | $T_{dissolution} = f(pH,\ temp,\ ionic\ strength)$ | Biopersistence/dissolution | hours/days | Nano-persistence |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note for Tables:** Because KTU exam answers are written by hand, the symbol $UF_{total}$ in cell 7 should be written as $UF_A \times UF_H \times UF_L \times UF_D$ in scripts. The vertical bar `|` symbol is avoided inside table cells to prevent markdown table breakage; use $\vert$ or $\mid$ when absolutely required.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industrial Utility

- **Regulatory decision-making**: EFSA, FDA, FSSAI, and Codex use these formulas to issue/no-issue market authorizations for nano-food products.
- **Product development by industry**: Companies like Nestlé, Unilever, and BASF must submit nano-specific dossiers including all four pillars before launching nano-encapsulated ingredients.
- **Occupational safety**: Used to set Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL) for factory workers handling ENM powders.
- **Environmental safety**: Predicts ENM loads in soil, water, and biosolids from nano-agrochemical runoff.
- **Public health policy**: Guides labeling requirements (e.g., EU Regulation 1169/2011 mandating "contains nano" labels).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **Hazard Identification** (qualitative — "is it harmful?") with **Hazard Characterization** (quantitative — "how much is harmful?"). Examiners frequently test this distinction for **7 marks**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) from the NOAEL

The NOAEL is the highest dose at which no statistically or biologically significant increase in adverse effects is observed in the most sensitive species. Because the NOAEL from an animal study is unlikely to directly reflect safe human exposure, **Uncertainty Factors (UF)** are applied.

**Step 1 — Identify the most sensitive species endpoint and read the NOAEL from the dose-response graph.**

$$ \text{Example (TiO}_2\ \text{ENM in rats, 90-day oral study): } NOAEL = 1000\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

**Step 2 — Apply interspecies uncertainty factor** ($UF_A = 10$): accounts for extrapolation from animal to human (pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic differences).

$$ NOAEL_{human-equivalent} = \frac{1000}{10} = 100\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

**Step 3 — Apply intraspecies uncertainty factor** ($UF_H = 10$): accounts for variability within the human population (age, sex, genetics, health status, children, pregnant women).

$$ NOAEL_{sensitive\ human} = \frac{100}{10} = 10\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

**Step 4 — Apply additional uncertainty factors if needed:**
- $UF_L = 1$ to 10: for LOAEL-to-NOAEL extrapolation
- $UF_D = 1$ to 10: for database deficiencies (e.g., absence of chronic or reproductive data)
- $UF_S = 1$ to 10: for sub-chronic to chronic extrapolation

**Step 5 — Combine all UFs multiplicatively** to obtain the total UF, then divide NOAEL by total UF:

$$ UF_{total} = UF_A \times UF_H \times UF_L \times UF_D = 10 \times 10 \times 1 \times 1 = 100 $$

$$ ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{UF_{total}} = \frac{1000}{100} = 10\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This ADI value means that a 70 kg adult can safely ingest up to $10 \times 70 = 700\ mg$ of TiO₂ ENM per day for a lifetime without appreciable health risk. **ADI is a chronic (lifetime) metric.**

### 3.2 Worked Example — Calculating the Hazard Quotient (HQ)

**Problem:** A nano-ZnO additive is permitted in breakfast cereal at a final concentration of **25 mg/kg food**. National consumption data show the average adult eats **0.30 kg cereal/day**. Oral absorption of nano-ZnO is measured at **0.04 (4%)**. Adult body weight = **70 kg**. The ADI for nano-ZnO established by EFSA is **0.3 mg/kg bw/day**. Calculate the HQ and interpret the result.

**Step 1 — Compute concentration ingested per kg body weight per day (EDD):**

$$ EDD = \frac{C \times IR \times ABS}{BW} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$ EDD = \frac{25\ \frac{mg}{kg\ food} \times 0.30\ \frac{kg\ food}{day} \times 0.04}{70\ kg} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**

$$ 25 \times 0.30 \times 0.04 = 0.30\ \frac{mg}{day} $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate final EDD:**

$$ EDD = \frac{0.30}{70} = 0.00429\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

**Step 5 — Compute Hazard Quotient:**

$$ HQ = \frac{EDD}{ADI} = \frac{0.00429}{0.3} = 0.0143 $$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

$$ HQ = 0.0143 \ll 1 \implies \text{Acceptable risk, no regulatory action required} $$

If we now assume **10% absorption** instead of 4% (worst-case assumption):

$$ EDD_{worst} = \frac{25 \times 0.30 \times 0.10}{70} = 0.0107\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

$$ HQ_{worst} = \frac{0.0107}{0.3} = 0.0357 \ll 1 \implies \text{Still acceptable} $$

Even in the worst case, HQ stays below 1.

### 3.3 Multi-Food Aggregate Exposure — Worked Example

If a consumer eats **three** nano-containing food products in one day:

$$ E_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{3} C_i \cdot IR_i \cdot ABS_i $$

$$ E_{total} = (20 \times 0.20 \times 0.05) + (15 \times 0.15 \times 0.08) + (10 \times 0.05 \times 0.10) $$

$$ E_{total} = 0.20 + 0.18 + 0.05 = 0.43\ \frac{mg}{day} $$

This is the **aggregated daily internal dose**; dividing by BW (70 kg) yields the EDD for risk characterization.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Standard Risk Approaches

| Approach | Output | Decision Rule | Applicable To | Key Weakness for ENMs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Quotient (HQ)** | Single number | $\leq 1$ acceptable | Threshold toxicants | Assumes single chemical entity |
| **Margin of Exposure (MoE)** | Single number | $\geq 10,000$ acceptable | Genotoxic carcinogens | Requires large animal dataset |
| **Benchmark Dose (BMD)** | BMDL₁₀ | Compare to EDD | All toxicants | Requires model fitting |
| **TTC (Threshold of Toxicological Concern)** | Exposure threshold | Below TTC = safe | Trace contaminants | Developed for chemicals, not particles |
| **IATA (Integrated Approaches to Testing & Assessment)** | Integrated evidence | Weight of evidence | ENMs (EFSA 2021) | Still maturing |

> [!TIP]
> For **nanomaterials**, the **IATA framework** (EFSA, 2021 guidance) is the current gold-standard because it accommodates nano-specific endpoints (protein corona, dissolution, agglomeration) that classical HQ/MoE cannot handle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four-Pillar Risk Assessment Framework for Food Nanomaterials

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENM in Food Product]:::input --> B[Pillar 1: Hazard Identification]
    B --> B1[Physicochemical Characterization]
    B --> B2[In vitro Assays]
    B --> B3[In vivo Animal Studies]
    B1 --> C[Pillar 2: Hazard Characterization]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C --> C1[Dose-Response Curve]
    C --> C2[Determine NOAEL or BMDL10]
    C2 --> D[Pillar 3: Exposure Assessment]
    D --> D1[Concentration in Food Matrix]
    D --> D2[Consumption Rate kg per day]
    D --> D3[Oral Absorption Fraction]
    D --> D4[Body Weight Normalization]
    D4 --> E[Pillar 4: Risk Characterization]
    E --> E1[Hazard Quotient HQ]
    E --> E2[Margin of Exposure MoE]
    E --> F[Risk Decision]:::decision
    F -->|HQ less than 1 OR MoE greater than 10000| G[Acceptable Risk]:::accept
    F -->|HQ greater than 1 OR MoE less than 10000| H[Unacceptable Risk]:::reject
    H --> I[Risk Management Measures]
    I --> I1[Reformulation]
    I --> I2[Labeling Mandate]
    I --> I3[Withdrawal]

    classDef input fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,stroke-width:2px,color:#0d47a1
    classDef decision fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,stroke-width:3px,color:#f57f17

    classDef reject fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#b71c1c
```

### 4.2 Exposure Routes and Toxicokinetic Pathway of Ingested ENMs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Food Containing ENM]:::source --> B[Oral Cavity]
    B --> C[Stomach pH 1 to 3]
    C --> D[Small Intestine]
    D --> D1[M Cell Uptake]
    D --> D2[Enterocyte Transcytosis]
    D --> D3[Paracellular Permeation]
    D1 --> E[Submucosa]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F1[Bloodstream]
    E --> F2[Lacteals Lymphatic]
    F1 --> G1[Liver Kupffer Cells]
    F1 --> G2[Spleen]
    F1 --> G3[Kidney Filtration]
    F1 --> G4[Brain BBB Crossing]
    F1 --> G5[Fetal Placenta]
    G1 --> H[RES Retention]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> I[Urinary Excretion]
    H --> J[Chronic Accumulation]

```

### 4.3 Iterative Risk Management Cycle (Post-Assessment)

```mermaid

    A[Risk Assessment Output] --> B[Risk Management Decision]
    B --> C{Acceptable Risk?}

    H --> I
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short Answers)**

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define Risk Assessment in the context of food nanotechnology. State the four pillars as per the NRC framework.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Risk Assessment is a systematic, science-based evaluation of the probability and severity of adverse effects caused by **engineered nanomaterials (ENMs)** intentionally used in food and food contact materials. The four pillars established by the **National Research Council (1983)** and adopted by EFSA are:
1. **Hazard Identification** — determining whether the ENM has intrinsic harmful properties
2. **Hazard Characterization** — quantifying the dose-response relationship (NOAEL, BMDL)
3. **Exposure Assessment** — estimating the actual daily human intake via ingestion
4. **Risk Characterization** — combining hazard and exposure to produce a risk estimate (HQ or MoE)

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for naming the framework, 1 mark for the four pillars with one-line explanation each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]** Distinguish between **Hazard** and **Risk** with one food-nanotechnology example each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Hazard | Risk |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Intrinsic capacity to cause harm | Probability of harm under specific exposure conditions |
| Depends on | Material's properties (chemistry, size, surface) | Hazard × Exposure |
| Quantified by | Toxicological studies (NOAEL, LD₅₀) | HQ, MoE, RQ |
| Example (ENM) | Nano-Ag can disrupt bacterial cell membranes | Risk of consuming bread with nano-Ag depends on its concentration and daily bread intake |

*If a material has hazard but exposure is zero, risk is zero. (1 mark each for correct distinction, 1 mark for valid nano-food example.)*

---

### **PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice — Choose Either A or B)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Computational & Conceptual*

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A food manufacturer proposes to use **nano-TiO₂ (E171-grade)** as a whitening agent in a powdered beverage mix at a concentration of **50 mg/kg**. EFSA has set the ADI for nano-TiO₂ at **0.10 mg/kg body weight/day** based on a NOAEL of **1000 mg/kg bw/day** in a 90-day rat study. The average adult consumption of the beverage is **0.25 kg/day**, oral absorption fraction is **0.001 (0.1%)**, and adult body weight is **70 kg**. **(7 marks)**

(i) Calculate the Estimated Daily Dose (EDD) for an average adult.
(ii) Calculate the Hazard Quotient (HQ) and interpret the result.
(iii) Identify which uncertainty factors were applied if the total UF = 1000 and compute ADI from the NOAEL.

**(b)** Explain the **IATA (Integrated Approaches to Testing and Assessment)** framework recommended by EFSA (2021) for nanomaterials, and discuss why classical HQ/MoE approaches are insufficient for ENMs. Mention at least **four nano-specific endpoints** that IATA incorporates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) EDD Calculation (3 marks)**

$$ EDD = \frac{C \times IR \times ABS}{BW} $$

$$ EDD = \frac{50\ \frac{mg}{kg} \times 0.25\ \frac{kg}{day} \times 0.001}{70\ kg} $$

$$ EDD = \frac{0.0125}{70} = 1.786 \times 10^{-4}\ \frac{mg}{kg\ bw \cdot day} $$

**(a) (ii) HQ Calculation and Interpretation (2 marks)**

$$ HQ = \frac{EDD}{ADI} = \frac{1.786 \times 10^{-4}}{0.10} = 1.786 \times 10^{-3} $$

$$ HQ = 0.00179 \ll 1 \implies \text{Acceptable risk; the proposed use is safe for the average adult} $$

**(a) (iii) Uncertainty Factor Analysis (2 marks)**

$$ ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{UF_{total}} \implies 0.10 = \frac{1000}{UF_{total}} $$

$$ UF_{total} = \frac{1000}{0.10} = 10000 $$

A UF of 10,000 implies the EFSA applied:
- $UF_A = 10$ (interspecies, animal-to-human)
- $UF_H = 10$ (intraspecies, human variability)
- $UF_L = 10$ (LOAEL-to-NOAEL extrapolation, since NOAEL was not directly observed)
- $UF_D = 10$ (database deficiency, e.g., absence of chronic/reproductive data)
- **Total UF** = $10 \times 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 10000$ ✓

**(b) IATA Framework for Nanomaterials (7 marks)**

The **IATA** is a structured, weight-of-evidence approach that combines physicochemical characterization, in silico, in vitro, in vivo, and omics data to produce a holistic risk decision, replacing the rigid one-test-one-decision paradigm of classical toxicology.

**Why classical HQ/MoE are insufficient for ENMs (4 marks):**
1. Classical approaches assume a single, stable chemical entity; ENMs **transform** (dissolve, agglomerate, lose coating) in food matrices and GI tract.
2. Classical toxicokinetics cannot describe **particle-specific distribution** to RES, brain, or placenta.
3. HQ/MoE use **mass-based** dose metrics; ENMs require **particle number, surface area, or surface reactivity** as dose metrics.
4. Classical approaches ignore **protein corona** formation that redefines the biological identity of the ENM.
5. Classical approaches lack standardized protocols for nano-specific endpoints like dissolution rate and biopersistence.

**Four nano-specific endpoints IATA incorporates (3 marks):**
1. **Protein corona composition** — proteomic identification of adsorbed biomolecules
2. **Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) generation** — DCFH-DA, EPR spectroscopy
3. **Cell membrane interaction and frustrated phagocytosis** — barrier integrity assays
4. **In vivo biopersistence and biodistribution** — ICP-MS, radiolabeling, electron microscopy
5. **Genotoxicity via comet and micronucleus assays** specifically optimized for nano-particles
6. **Dissolution rate in simulated GI fluids** — critical for grouping by solubility

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual & Regulatory*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **four pillars of risk assessment** as applied to engineered nanomaterials in food, with specific emphasis on the in vitro and in vivo assays used at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the regulatory approaches of **EFSA, US FDA, and Codex Alimentarius** for nanomaterial risk assessment, highlighting nano-specific provisions and at least three challenges unique to nanomaterials. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Four Pillars (7 marks)**

**Pillar 1 — Hazard Identification (2 marks):**
- Physicochemical characterization: TEM/DLS for size, BET for surface area, XRD for crystallinity, zeta-potential for surface charge.
- In vitro assays: MTT (mitochondrial viability), LDH (membrane integrity), DCFH-DA (ROS), Comet assay (DNA damage), Micronucleus (clastogenicity).
- In vivo: acute oral toxicity (OECD TG 423, 425), 28-day, 90-day sub-chronic (OECD TG 408).

**Pillar 2 — Hazard Characterization (2 marks):**
- Dose-response curves; identification of NOAEL and LOAEL; modern use of **Benchmark Dose (BMD)** modeling.
- Calculation of ADI/TDI/RfD using uncertainty factors $UF = 100$ or $1000$.

**Pillar 3 — Exposure Assessment (1.5 marks):**
- Concentration in food ($C_i$), ingestion rate ($IR_i$), absorption ($ABS_i$), body weight ($BW$).
- Aggregate and cumulative exposure across multiple foods; chronic vs. acute scenarios.

**Pillar 4 — Risk Characterization (1.5 marks):**
- HQ for threshold effects, MoE for non-threshold (genotoxic carcinogens).
- IATA for ENMs; uncertainty and weight-of-evidence reporting.

**(b) Regulatory Comparison (7 marks)**

**EFSA (European Food Safety Authority) — 2021 Nano-Guidance (2.5 marks):**
- Mandates pre-market nano-specific safety assessment.
- Requires physicochemical characterization, ADME studies, and IATA.
- EU Regulation 1169/2011 requires "**contains nano**" label on consumer products.

**US FDA — 2014 and 2022 Guidance (2 marks):**
- "**Pre-market safety assessment for nanotechnology**" — case-by-case evaluation.
- Focus on **size, properties, and effects** rather than a strict nano-definition.
- Continues to evolve with the National Nanotechnology Initiative (NNI).

**Codex Alimentarius Commission (FAO/WHO) (1 mark):**
- 2019 and 2023 documents: principles for risk analysis of foods derived from nanotechnology.
- Calls for international harmonization, transparency, and capacity-building.

**Three Nano-Specific Challenges (1.5 marks):**
1. **Analytical detection** — most ENMs cannot be detected at low concentrations in complex food matrices (no validated methods).
2. **Dosimetry ambiguity** — should dose be expressed in mass, particle number, or surface area?
3. **Transformation in food** — ENMs may agglomerate, dissolve, or react with food components, changing their toxicity during processing or digestion.
4. **Lack of long-term chronic data** — most published studies are 28 or 90 days; lifetime carcinogenicity data are scarce.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most frequently lose marks on Risk Assessment questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Risk Assessment with Risk Management** — RA is the *scientific* part; Risk Management is the *policy* part. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting body weight in EDD formula** — examiners specifically check whether you divided by 70 kg. Forgetting BW = -2 marks.
> 3. **Writing the wrong UF default** — the default $UF_{total}$ for an acceptable NOAEL-derived ADI is **100** (10×10), not 10 or 1000 unless the situation justifies it.
> 4. **Mass-based dose only** — for ENMs, examiners now expect you to mention that **surface area or particle number** may be a more appropriate dose metric. Omitting this = -1 mark on Part B.
> 5. **Calling IATA a "test"** — IATA is a *framework* that integrates tests, not a single test. Calling it a test = -1 mark.
> 6. **Ignoring aggregation across foods** — modern RA requires summing exposure across all foods containing the ENM.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- **Risk = Hazard × Exposure** is the master identity. Memorize it verbatim.
- The **NRC four-pillar framework** is the global standard: Hazard ID → Hazard Char → Exposure → Risk Char.
- **NOAEL, LOAEL, ADI, TDI, RfD** all have units of **mg/kg body weight/day** — never write them in mg/day.
- The **default Uncertainty Factor** is **100** (interspecies 10 × intraspecies 10). Use 1000 if the NOAEL is replaced by a LOAEL or if database is deficient.
- **HQ ≤ 1** = acceptable; **HQ > 1** = unacceptable (threshold toxicants).
- **MoE ≥ 10,000** = acceptable; **MoE < 10,000** = unacceptable (genotoxic carcinogens, EFSA default).
- **EDD formula** = $\frac{C \times IR \times ABS}{BW}$ — always include the body weight.
- For **ENMs**, classical mass-based HQ/MoE may be inadequate — EFSA 2021 recommends the **IATA framework** with nano-specific endpoints.
- **Nano-specific hazard concepts** (must know): protein corona, frustrated phagocytosis, Trojan horse effect, ROS generation, agglomeration, biopersistence.
- **Regulatory bodies**: EFSA (EU), US FDA, Codex Alimentarius (international), FSSAI (India), FSANZ (Australia/NZ).
- **Standard body weights**: 70 kg adult, 15 kg child (EFSA default).
- **Exposure aggregation** across multiple foods is mandatory in modern assessments.
- **Oral absorption fraction (ABS)** for most ENMs is **very low** (0.001–0.05), often making HQ very small — but do not ignore biopersistence and RES accumulation.
- **Dose metric debate**: mass vs. particle number vs. surface area — examiners expect you to acknowledge this.
- **IATA** = Integrated Approaches to Testing and Assessment (EFSA 2021) — integrates in silico, in vitro, in vivo, and omics into a weight-of-evidence decision.
- **Frustrated phagocytosis** is the *unique* hazard of high-aspect-ratio nanomaterials (HARN) such as nanofibers, nanotubes, and nanorods.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toxicological Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT743 - NANOTECHNOLOGY IN FOOD
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Toxicological Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Toxicology** is the scientific discipline that studies the adverse effects of chemical, physical, or biological agents on living organisms and ecosystems, including the mechanisms of action, exposure assessment, dose–response relationships, and risk characterization.

**Nanotoxicology** is the specialized sub-discipline of toxicology that investigates the toxicological profile of materials possessing at least one dimension in the nanoscale range (typically **1–100 nm**). Within the domain of *Nanotechnology in Food*, toxicological studies evaluate the safety of engineered nanomaterials (ENMs) intentionally added to food products (e.g., nano-TiO₂ as a whitener, nano-silver as an antimicrobial) or those that may incidentally migrate from food-contact materials (e.g., nano-clays in packaging).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> For Module 2, the university expects students to clearly distinguish between *bulk-material toxicity* and *nanomaterial toxicity*. Bulk TiO₂ is classified as "Generally Recognized As Safe" (GRAS), but its nano-form exhibits significantly different biological behavior. This size-induced toxicity shift is the **single most exam-relevant concept** in this topic.

## 1.2 Core Principles of Nanotoxicology

The toxicological evaluation of food-grade nanomaterials rests on four foundational pillars:

1. **Exposure Assessment** — Quantifying how much of the nanomaterial reaches the consumer (ingestion dose, frequency, duration).
2. **Hazard Identification** — Determining the inherent biological harm potential of the nanomaterial.
3. **Dose–Response Analysis** — Establishing the relationship between exposure dose and the magnitude of the adverse effect.
4. **Risk Characterization** — Integrating the above into a quantitative risk metric for regulatory decision-making.

## 1.3 The Triad of Nanomaterial Toxicity Drivers

Unlike conventional chemicals, nanomaterial toxicity is governed by **physicochemical properties** that often dominate over chemical composition:

| Property | Biological Consequence |
|----------|------------------------|
| **Particle size (1–100 nm)** | Enhanced cellular uptake, blood–brain barrier penetration, lymphatic translocation |
| **Surface area-to-volume ratio** | Higher catalytic/reactivity, increased ROS generation per unit mass |
| **Surface chemistry (charge, coating, functionalization)** | Protein corona formation, immune recognition, agglomeration |
| **Shape (spheres, rods, fibers, tubes)** | Fiber-shaped materials exhibit asbestos-like pathogenicity (frustrated phagocytosis) |
| **Crystalline phase** | Anatase TiO₂ is more cytotoxic than rutile TiO₂ at the nanoscale |
| **Agglomeration state** | Determines effective vs. administered dose; agglomerates may behave as bulk |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Razor-Sharp Sugar Cube":**
> Imagine a sugar cube and a packet of razor blades of identical *mass*. A child who ingests the sugar cube experiences no harm. The same mass of razor blades, however, can cause severe lacerations. Nanoparticles follow a similar logic: **mass alone is a poor predictor of toxicity**; *surface reactivity* and *physical form* drive biological damage. A 50 mg dose of bulk TiO₂ is harmless, but 50 mg of nano-TiO₂ has roughly **40–60 times more reactive surface area**, dramatically amplifying its biological impact.

## 1.4 Routes of Exposure in Food Nanotechnology

| Route | Example | Relevance |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| **Oral (gastrointestinal)** | Direct ingestion of nano-encapsulated nutrients, food colorants | Primary route for food-grade ENMs |
| **Inhalation** | Aerosolized nano-powders during food processing/packaging | Occupational exposure for factory workers |
| **Dermal** | Migration from nano-coated packaging into skin | Relevant for food handlers |
| **Translocation** | GI-absorbed NPs reaching systemic circulation → secondary organs | Critical for long-term chronic toxicity |

## 1.5 Key Toxicological Endpoints

- **Cytotoxicity** — Damage or death of individual cells (measured via MTT, LDH assays).
- **Genotoxicity** — Damage to genetic material (DNA strand breaks, mutations; Ames test, comet assay).
- **Immunotoxicity** — Dysregulation of immune responses (allergenicity, inflammation).
- **Reproductive & Developmental Toxicity** — Effects on gametes, embryos, and fetal development.
- **Carcinogenicity** — Potential to induce cancer (long-term rodent bioassays).
- **Neurotoxicity** — Adverse effects on the nervous system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dose–Response Paradigm in Nanotoxicology

The dose–response relationship is the cornerstone of toxicology. For nanomaterials, the conventional **mass-based dose (mg/kg body weight)** is supplemented by additional dose metrics that often better predict biological response.

### 2.1.1 Types of Dose Metrics

1. **Mass Dose ($D_m$)** — Most commonly reported; measured in mg/kg.
2. **Particle Number Dose ($D_N$)** — Number of particles per unit mass of organism; highly relevant for nanoparticles because particle *count* scales with the *cube* of diameter.
3. **Surface Area Dose ($D_{SA}$)** — Total particle surface area per unit mass of organism; considered by many researchers as the *most predictive* dose metric for nanoparticle toxicity.

### 2.1.2 Critical Toxicological Thresholds

| Parameter | Definition | KTU Significance |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose causing 50% mortality in test population | Acute toxicity benchmark |
| **NOAEL** | No Observed Adverse Effect Level — highest dose with no statistically significant adverse effect | Used to calculate Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) |
| **LOAEL** | Lowest Observed Adverse Effect Level — lowest dose producing a statistically significant adverse effect | Lower bound of toxicity |
| **ADI** | Acceptable Daily Intake = $\text{NOAEL} / \text{Safety Factor}$ | Regulatory intake limit (mg/kg/day) |
| **TDI** | Tolerable Daily Intake | Used for contaminants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** For nanoparticles, the safety factor in ADI calculations is often set at **100–1000** because of the higher uncertainty in nanomaterial toxicokinetics (default factor of 100 for inter- and intra-species variation, plus an additional factor of 10 for nanomaterial-specific uncertainties per EFSA 2011 guidance).

## 2.2 Mechanisms of Nanomaterial Toxicity

The biological harm caused by food-grade nanomaterials is exerted through **four principal mechanistic pathways**:

### 2.2.1 ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) Generation — "Oxidative Stress Pathway"

Nanoparticles, particularly metal/metal-oxide ENMs (ZnO, TiO₂, CuO, Ag), catalyze the formation of reactive oxygen species on their high-energy surfaces:

$$\text{O}_2 + e^- \xrightarrow{\text{NP surface}} \text{O}_2^{\bullet -} \xrightarrow{\text{SOD}} \text{H}_2\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Fenton}} {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

These radicals attack:
- **Lipids** → lipid peroxidation → membrane damage
- **Proteins** → thiol oxidation → enzyme inactivation
- **DNA** → strand breaks → mutagenesis

### 2.2.2 Membrane and Organelle Damage

Sharp-edged or needle-like nanoparticles (e.g., multi-walled carbon nanotubes) can physically puncture cell membranes, lysosomes, and mitochondrial membranes, causing necrosis and release of intracellular contents.

### 2.2.3 Protein Corona and Biomolecular Hijacking

Upon entering biological fluids, nanoparticles adsorb a layer of proteins, forming the **"protein corona"**. This corona:
- Alters the *biological identity* of the nanoparticle.
- Can trigger immune recognition (opsonization) → rapid clearance.
- Can dysregulate native proteins (e.g., β-amyloid fibrillation kinetics).

### 2.2.4 Genotoxicity — Direct and Indirect

- **Direct genotoxicity:** Nanoparticles physically interact with DNA or spindle apparatus during mitosis.
- **Indirect genotoxicity:** Via ROS generation, inflammation-driven oxidative DNA damage, or interference with DNA repair machinery.

## 2.3 ADME of Food-Grade Nanomaterials

**ADME** = **A**bsorption, **D**istribution, **M**etabolism, **E**xcretion — adapted for nanomaterials (sometimes called *nano-ADME* or *toxicokinetics*).

| Stage | Nanomaterial-Specific Considerations |
|-------|--------------------------------------|
| **Absorption** | GI uptake of particles < 200 nm is dramatically enhanced via M-cells of Peyer's patches and enterocyte transcytosis. Bioavailability of bulk TiO₂ ≈ 0.1%, but nano-TiO₂ may reach 1–4%. |
| **Distribution** | Once in systemic circulation, NPs distribute to liver (Kupffer cells) > spleen > kidney > lung > brain. Particles < 50 nm can cross the blood–brain barrier. |
| **Metabolism (Biotransformation)** | Most ENMs are **not metabolized** in the classical sense. They may undergo dissolution (ZnO → Zn²⁺), corrosion, sulfidation (Ag → Ag₂S), or corona remodeling. |
| **Excretion** | Hepatobiliary route is dominant for particles > 6 nm. Renal clearance is efficient only for particles < 5.5 nm (glomerular filtration cutoff). Larger particles may persist in tissue for months (long-term accumulation). |

## 2.4 In Vitro vs. In Vivo Testing Frameworks

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** A frequent 7-mark question asks students to *compare* in vitro and in vivo toxicological methods. Memorize the following table.

| Feature | In Vitro (Cell-based) | In Vivo (Animal/Rodent) |
|---------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| **Cost & Time** | Low cost, rapid (hours–days) | High cost, long (months–years) |
| **Ethical Concern** | Minimal (3R principle compliant) | Significant (requires IACUC approval) |
| **Endpoints** | Cytotoxicity, ROS, genotoxicity, inflammatory markers | Systemic toxicity, organ pathology, behavioral changes |
| **Throughput** | High — amenable to high-content screening | Low |
| **Relevance** | Limited — lacks systemic complexity | Gold standard for human risk extrapolation |
| **Dose Metric** | Surface area dose is most predictive | Mass dose traditionally reported |
| **Common Assays** | MTT, LDH, comet, Ames, micronucleus | OECD TG 408, 451 (carcinogenicity), 414 (developmental) |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Toxicological Studies

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **Mass Dose** | $D_m = \dfrac{m_{\text{NP}}}{m_{\text{body}}}$ | mg/kg | Standard dose metric |
| 2 | **Particle Number Dose** | $D_N = \dfrac{N_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{body}}}$ | particles/kg | Better for low-mass/high-count NPs |
| 3 | **Particle Number from Mass** | $N = \dfrac{6 m}{\pi d^3 \rho}$ | dimensionless | Converts mass to particle count |
| 4 | **Surface Area Dose** | $D_{SA} = \dfrac{N \cdot \pi d^2}{m_{\text{body}}} = \dfrac{6 m}{\rho \, d \, m_{\text{body}}}$ | m²/kg | Best predictor of nanotoxicity |
| 5 | **Specific Surface Area** | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{\rho \, d}$ | m²/g | Material property |
| 6 | **Acceptable Daily Intake** | $ADI = \dfrac{\text{NOAEL}}{SF}$ | mg/kg/day | Regulatory limit |
| 7 | **Safety Factor (nano-default)** | $SF = 100 \times UF_n$ where $UF_n = 10$ | dimensionless | EFSA nanomaterial guidance |
| 8 | **Renal Filtration Cutoff** | $d_{\text{cutoff}} \approx 5.5$ nm | nm | Particles smaller cleared renally |
| 9 | **GI Uptake Threshold** | $d_{\text{enhanced}} < 200$ nm | nm | Enhanced uptake below this size |
| 10 | **Reactive Oxygen Species Yield** | $\text{ROS yield} \propto \dfrac{SSA \times [\text{defect density}]}{d}$ | a.u. | Predictive toxicological index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** When writing absolute values or magnitude in KTU exams, **always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$** — never use the vertical pipe `|` character, as it will break markdown table rendering and is a common typographical error noted in KTU valuation scripts.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles discussed above are not merely academic — they are operationally critical in:

- **Regulatory dossier preparation** (EFSA, FDA, FSSAI) for novel food additives containing nanomaterials.
- **Design of safer-by-design nanomaterials** (e.g., SiO₂ nanoparticles coated with food-grade polymers to reduce membrane damage).
- **Industrial hygiene** in food processing plants handling nano-powders.
- **Setting maximum residue limits (MRLs)** for nano-pesticides in agricultural produce.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Number of Nanoparticles from Mass

This is one of the most frequently tested derivations in KTU examinations. Let us derive the expression for the number of spherical nanoparticles contained in a known mass.

**Given:** A spherical nanoparticle of diameter $d$ and material density $\rho$.

**Step 1 — Volume of a single spherical nanoparticle:**

$$v_{\text{single}} = \frac{4}{3} \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{\pi d^3}{6}$$

**Step 2 — Mass of a single nanoparticle:**

$$m_{\text{single}} = v_{\text{single}} \times \rho = \frac{\pi d^3 \rho}{6}$$

**Step 3 — Total number of nanoparticles in a sample of mass $m$:**

$$N = \frac{m}{m_{\text{single}}} = \frac{m}{\frac{\pi d^3 \rho}{6}} = \frac{6m}{\pi d^3 \rho}$$

$$\boxed{N = \frac{6m}{\pi d^3 \rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation:** Notice that $N \propto \dfrac{1}{d^3}$. This means a **2-fold decrease in particle diameter produces an 8-fold increase in particle number** for the same mass — explaining why nano-dosing can be so much more biologically intense than equivalent bulk dosing.

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Surface Area of a Nanoparticle

**Step 1 — Surface area of a single sphere:**

$$A_{\text{single}} = 4\pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^2 = \pi d^2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of a single sphere** (as derived above): $m_{\text{single}} = \dfrac{\pi d^3 \rho}{6}$

**Step 3 — Specific Surface Area (SSA):**

$$SSA = \frac{A_{\text{single}}}{m_{\text{single}}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\frac{\pi d^3 \rho}{6}} = \frac{6}{\rho d}$$

$$\boxed{SSA = \frac{6}{\rho d} \;\; \text{[m}^2/\text{g]}}$$

**Step 4 — Implication for nanotoxicology:** SSA scales as $1/d$. Halving the diameter **doubles the specific surface area**, which directly amplifies ROS generation potential and surface-mediated biological interactions.

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Mass-to-Number Conversion (Board-Pattern Question)

> **Problem (14-mark adapted):** A food-grade ZnO nanopowder sample has a mass of 5 mg. The particles are spherical with an average diameter of 30 nm. The density of ZnO is 5.61 g/cm³. Calculate:
> (a) the number of nanoparticles in the sample.
> (b) the total surface area.
> (c) the specific surface area.
> (d) comment on the toxicological implication if this is orally administered to a 70 kg adult at this single dose.

**Solution:**

**(a) Number of nanoparticles:**

Convert all units to SI (or use CGS consistently). Let us work in CGS (cm, g):

$$d = 30 \text{ nm} = 30 \times 10^{-7} \text{ cm} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}$$

$$m = 5 \text{ mg} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g}, \quad \rho = 5.61 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$N = \frac{6m}{\pi d^3 \rho} = \frac{6 \times (5 \times 10^{-3})}{\pi \times (3.0 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 5.61}$$

Denominator:

$$\pi \times (3.0 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 5.61 = \pi \times 2.7 \times 10^{-17} \times 5.61$$

$$= 3.1416 \times 1.5147 \times 10^{-16} = 4.758 \times 10^{-16} \text{ g}$$

Therefore:

$$N = \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-2}}{4.758 \times 10^{-16}} = 6.305 \times 10^{13} \text{ particles}$$

**(b) Total Surface Area:**

$$A_{\text{total}} = N \times \pi d^2$$

$$= 6.305 \times 10^{13} \times \pi \times (3.0 \times 10^{-6})^2 \text{ cm}^2$$

$$= 6.305 \times 10^{13} \times \pi \times 9.0 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$= 6.305 \times 10^{13} \times 2.827 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$= 1.782 \text{ cm}^2 \times 10^{3} = 1.78 \times 10^{3} \text{ cm}^2$$

$$A_{\text{total}} \approx 0.178 \text{ m}^2$$

**(c) Specific Surface Area:**

$$SSA = \frac{6}{\rho d} = \frac{6}{5.61 \times 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ cm}}$$

$$= \frac{6}{1.683 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.565 \times 10^{5} \text{ cm}^2/\text{g} = 35.65 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$$

**(d) Toxicological Implication:**

Dose per kg body weight:

$$D_m = \frac{5 \text{ mg}}{70 \text{ kg}} \approx 0.071 \text{ mg/kg}$$

The total reactive surface area delivered per kg is:

$$D_{SA} = \frac{A_{\text{total}}}{m_{\text{body}}} = \frac{0.178 \text{ m}^2}{70 \text{ kg}} \approx 2.55 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2/\text{kg} = 2.55 \text{ cm}^2/\text{kg}$$

Although the mass dose is small, the **per-particle number dose is enormous** ($6.3 \times 10^{13}$ particles), and the high SSA means significant Zn²⁺ release and ROS generation potential in the GI tract. The **surface area dose is the more reliable toxicological indicator** for such a sample.

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — ADI Calculation with Nanomaterial Uncertainty Factor

> **Problem:** A 90-day rodent feeding study identifies a NOAEL of 50 mg/kg/day for a food-grade nano-iron supplement. Using EFSA's default uncertainty factors (100 for inter- and intra-species variation, plus an additional 10-fold for nanomaterial uncertainties), calculate the ADI.

**Solution:**

$$SF = 100 \times 10 = 1000$$

$$ADI = \frac{NOAEL}{SF} = \frac{50 \text{ mg/kg/day}}{1000} = 0.05 \text{ mg/kg/day}$$

For a 70 kg adult, the maximum daily intake would be:

$$\text{ADI}_{70\text{kg}} = 0.05 \times 70 = 3.5 \text{ mg/day}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget to apply the **additional 10× uncertainty factor** specific to nanomaterials, leading to an overestimation of ADI by an order of magnitude. KTU examiners frequently deduct 2 marks specifically for this oversight.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Particle Size vs. Toxicological Consequence

| Diameter Range | Predominant Biological Behavior | Toxicological Concern |
|---------------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **> 1 µm** | Bulk-like behavior; minimal cellular uptake | Low — behaves as conventional particle |
| **100 nm – 1 µm** | Phagocytosed by macrophages; trapped in mucosa | Granuloma formation, local inflammation |
| **50–100 nm** | Endocytosed by non-phagocytic cells; enters lymphatics | Systemic distribution, chronic accumulation |
| **20–50 nm** | Penetrates deep tissue, may cross blood–brain barrier | Neurotoxicity, placental transfer |
| **5.5–20 nm** | Rapid renal clearance; high surface reactivity | Acute ROS bursts in renal epithelium |
| **< 5.5 nm** | Efficiently filtered by glomeruli; excreted in urine | Lower bioaccumulation but high local tubular toxicity |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
